title: Tiruchi Means a Turning-Point Conference, Does It Not? (7)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source images were used to resolve corrupted names and phrases in the programme and procession passages, including Sathyavani Muthu, K. Ponmudy, seventy speakers, and `தளகர்த்தர்கள்` (commanders). The printed salutation and only source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR/page-break repairs are restored in the Tamil reading copy. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: In my previous six "Udanpirappu" letters, I described the DMK's conferences through its sixth general State conference. The party's seventh general State conference and anti-Hindi conference were held together on 26, 27 and 28 March 1993 at Perarignar Anna Nagar in Coimbatore, in the Thalamuthu Natarajan pavilion, with the Valparai Ramasamy facade and the Coimbatore Aranganathan hall. I presided over the conference and raised the flag. General Secretary Professor inaugurated it. The Reception Committee Chairman was my dear friend M. Kannappan, then the DMK's Coimbatore district secretary.

Musical programmes were presented by Isai Murasu Nagore Hanifa, Mayilaithevan, Murasoli Mugilan, Vijaya Thayanban, Valavanur Manimaran, Nellai Abubacker, Arur Senthamizhan, Rajapalayam Isaimani and Thousand Lights S. A. M. Hussain Abdul Khader. On the first night, Police Kannan's troupe staged the play "The Dawn Rising in the West," and on the second, N. S. K. Dhamu's troupe staged "Run and Play, Child."

Next, the DMK's eighth general State conference was held here in Tiruchi on 26, 27 and 28 January 1996. Arranged magnificently by today's Reception Committee Chairman K. N. Nehru, it took place in the Anbil Dharmalingam pavilion, with the Mannai Narayanasamy facade, the Sadiq Batcha hall, the Thennarasu Martyrs' Memorial and the Veeraiya memorial flag dais. This was followed by the special State conference in Salem on 27, 28 and 29 June 1997; the special inaugural conference of the DMK's golden jubilee in Tirunelveli on 17 and 18 September 1998; the Villupuram district DMK conference on 19, 20 and 21 September 2003; the southern-region DMK conference in Virudhunagar on 21 and 22 February 2004; a special DMK conference in Salem on 21 and 22 August 2004 combining my diamond-jubilee celebration and the celebration of the parliamentary-election victory; the Dindigul regional conference on 12 and 13 March 2005; the Thanjavur regional conference on 16 and 17 April 2005; the Coimbatore regional conference on 28 and 29 May 2005; and the Vellore regional conference on 27 and 28 August 2005. Thereafter, the DMK's ninth general State conference was held with great distinction here in Tiruchi on 3, 4 and 5 March 2006. Mrs Sonia Gandhi, president of the All India Congress Committee and guiding leader of the United Progressive Alliance government, participated.

That was followed by the DMK Youth Wing's first State conference in Tirunelveli on 15 and 16 December 2007 and the DMK Women's Wing State conference on 14 and 15 June 2008. It is after these that our State conference will be held in Tiruchi on the coming 15th and 16th.

Only five days remain between this letter and the conference. All of you will be preparing to come with your families. Eight years after the ninth DMK State conference attended by Mrs Sonia Gandhi was held here in Tiruchi, this tenth State conference is taking place. I have written seven letters inviting you to this conference with affection and rightful intimacy: come, come! I remember writing eleven letters for the eighth State conference. Speaking at that conference, I said: "No one can deny that Reception Committee Chairman, younger brother Nehru, bears full responsibility for its success. I cannot forget the hardships he has endured and the work he has done. Not only does he telephone Chennai from Tiruchi every day; if he hears some news in the morning, he drives to Chennai that evening, consults me, the General Secretary and the Treasurer in person, and immediately rushes back to Tiruchi to continue the work." Through the preparations for this conference, he has proved those words true once again.

A conference always brings me joy. But this is a State conference, and in Tiruchi. What limit could there be to my happiness? Tiruchi awakens so many memories in me. Those memories, brimming with tenderness and emotion, have circled and spun through my heart over the past few days, casting their light. My beloved friend Anbil Dharmalingam lived in this city as my friend, my brother, my companion in life and one who took pride in calling himself my bodyguard. Whenever I had an event in Tiruchi or Thanjavur district, Anbil would stand on one side of the stage and Mannai Narayanasamy, who was like a mother to me, would sit on the other. Memories of them have risen in wave after wave within me over the past several weeks and shaken me deeply.

On 21 February 2006, I came with younger brother Stalin, then a Deputy General Secretary of the party, and headquarters Principal Secretary Duraimurugan to inspect arrangements for the ninth State conference held here on 3, 4 and 5 March. It was the conference at which the number of constituencies to be contested by the DMK and its allied parties was to be announced. I raised the flag on an eighty-two-foot pole. I was then eighty-two years old. At this tenth State conference, as its first event on the morning of 15 February, I will raise the flag on a ninety-foot pole.

The 2006 ninth State conference here in Tiruchi was held at Anna Nagar: the pavilion named for Anbil Poyyamozhi; the stage for Murasoli Maran; the hall for Thu. Pa. Azhagamuthu; the outer facade for S. J. Sadiq Batcha; the inner facade for K. So. Ganesan; the entrance for J. S. Rasu; the flag dais for Singath Thamizhan Chinnasamy; and the self-respect flame tower for Nanjilar.

For this tenth State conference in 2014, the pavilion is named for Veerapandi S. Arumugam; the stage for Vasuki Murugesan; the hall for Porchelvi Ilamurugu; the front facade for M. S. Venkatachalam; the outer facade for Tha. Kiruttinan; the inner facade for Ma. Balakrishnan; the entrance for Poondi K. Kalaichelvan; and the flag dais for K. N. Ramajayam.

On the first day, younger brother K. N. Nehru will deliver his welcome address as Reception Committee Chairman, followed by the proposal and seconding of the conference president. Portraits of Thanthai Periyar, Perarignar Anna, Sir Pitti Theagaraya, T. M. Nair, Dr Natesan, the Revolutionary Poet, Moovalur Moothattiyar, Mrs Sathyavani Muthu and the martyrs of the language struggle will respectively be unveiled by Tiruchi Selvendran, Tiruchi N. Siva MP, Dr Sabapathy Mohan, Coimbatore M. Ramanathan, Alandur R. S. Bharathi, Kavichudar Kavithaipithan, Subbulakshmi Jagadeesan, M. A. Noorjahan Begum and Dr K. Ponmudy. In the evening, the party's front-ranking speakers will address various subjects. General Secretary Professor will conclude the first day's proceedings.

Speeches on various subjects will continue on the second morning. Resolutions will be adopted at about eleven. After addresses by Deputy General Secretaries Duraimurugan, V. P. Duraisamy and Sarguna Pandian, and Principal Secretary Arcot Veerasamy, party Treasurer Thalapathi M. K. Stalin will speak.

In the evening, leaders of the DMK's allied parties will speak: Tamilar Thalaivar K. Veeramani; Thol. Thirumavalavan MP; Professor K. M. Kader Mohideen; Professor M. H. Jawahirullah MLA; Dr K. Krishnasamy MLA; Arulalar R. M. Veerappan; Professor Suba. Veerapandian; Ku. Sellamuthu; Sridhar Vandayar; Pon. Kumar; Bishop Ezra Sargunam; L. Santhanam; N. R. Dhanapalan; Tiruppur Altaf; P. N. Ammasi; and Liaquat Ali Khan. At about seven, I will deliver the conference president's address.

Thirty-seven special speakers have assigned subjects; there are sixteen allied-party leaders, nine portrait-unveilers and the party's front-ranking figures. Altogether, about seventy people must speak during the two-day conference. It would therefore be best for speakers to be concise and illuminating. Many have already begun searching for and assembling ideas suited to their subjects. I therefore ask, with the rights affection gives me, that everyone speaking at the conference allot their time carefully and come prepared.

Only five days remain, just 120 hours. Ah! With my inner eye I see them: like the Chola with the tiger flag, the Pandya with the fish flag and the Chera with the bow flag who once ruled this ancient Tamil land with chariots, cavalry and a vast host, the commanders of our district organisations sit majestically in the front row; behind them, the activists of every district. I see the Youth Wing, Labour Wing, Women's Wing, Students' Wing, Literary Wing, Farmers' Wing, Volunteers' Wing, Advocates' Wing, Weavers' Wing, the Art, Literature and Rationalist Forum, Fishermen's Wing, Scheduled Castes Welfare Committee and Women's Volunteers' Wing, displaying their pageant of awakening and marching in with their secretaries as though floodgates had opened, as though rising in valour to enter the field and reap courage.

I see the lion cubs; the Tamil women descended from the warrior who drove away a charging tiger with her winnowing tray; the working class whose hands have hardened; the student treasures who, having studied history, come with heads held high to make new history in Tamil Nadu; the bearers of literature; the great farming community declaring, "We who plough and produce shall spread as the roots of the DMK"; the sacred community of weavers proclaiming, "We shall protect honour and the dignity of the Tamil people"; and comrades of the law marching to the conference to lash a dictatorial government that has bid farewell to the rule of law and wounded law and order at will. Seeing them all, I overflow with delight; I become a forest peacock dancing in joy.
Ah, how many wings! Every one a jewel adorning the heroic form of the DMK: gleaming golden gems, diamond gems!
When I call them gold, I mean their character; when I call them diamonds, I mean their resolve!

Udanpirappē, this conference scene I behold and rejoice in with my mind's eye is no more than the tip of a sesame seed beside the conference that you and I will witness in Tiruchi on 15 and 16 February.

It will be seven seas: a sea of awakening, a sea of waves, a roaring sea! Not one thousand, ten thousand, one lakh or one crore, but heads everywhere, as though countless lily buds have risen and turned Tiruchi into one vast pond. Heads! Heads! Heads!
You ask me with your eyes: "You are already dreaming in delight of what you will see five days from now. Will your dream come true?"

It will, Udanpirappē, it will! When I place my faith in the truth that all that happens is through you; when your gathering is a heroic host that returns carrying the tree when told merely to cut it, why should I hesitate to say, "It will"?

Shall I tell you something new? From my youth, I trained myself not to sleep for long, whether by day or night. During election campaigns, I would speak at meetings until dawn, go to bed at six in the morning, rise by seven and begin my work.

Perhaps it is because I have crossed ninety, but some recent events trouble me a little. Perhaps because pain grazes, unknown even to me, in some corner of my heart, even the sleep that comes in between is not sleep in which I forget myself. The phrases and descriptions I have written to you about the Tiruchi conference take shape and run like images across a coloured screen; they settle upon the screen of my thoughts and return again and again as beautiful dreams.

All these dreams I have seen will come true. Tiruchi will tremble. Ever since the State conference was announced, there has been fresh life, awakening and a blossoming of faces, not only among party treasures like you, but among every section of the Tamil people. They are expecting something. Can we give it to them?

Without you, without your labour and your energetic action, when have I ever imagined that I could act alone, dig open the earth and tear apart the sky? When have I ever said so? One can say a thing only if one has thought it!

My Udanpirappē, who understands this clearly, you are already travelling towards the Tiruchi conference. Though it is something I always say, I place it once more in your ears, trusting that neither you nor the Udanpirappugal travelling with you will disregard my word, and asking that you must not.

Whichever vehicle you choose, travel with the utmost care. There must be no needless speed, haste or agitation on the journey. Until you come, return and reach your town, I shall carry fire in my stomach. Only the news that you reached home without the slightest trouble will give me double joy and comfort. Rest at intervals along the way. I ask vehicle drivers too to act responsibly. Duty, dignity and discipline are of the greatest importance at the conference. Never forget that they must be guarded as more precious than life itself. Are we not gathering in Tiruchi to renew the Dravidian movement, strengthen it and carry it forward with resolve? Tiruchi has become a fortress of the brave! Awakening! Awakening! Awakening everywhere! This is Tiruchi today. Tomorrow we shall see Tamil Nadu blossom!
With affection,
M. K.
9 February 2014
